food_subgroup: Offal-based broth dishes / ethnic specialty hotpot

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Cow Bile Hotpot (牛瘪火锅). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is 'beef offal food product' (FOODON_03315595), which covers the primary protein component. The bile-infused broth and wild-herb components are not individually represented in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bovine meat products — offal and organ meats, cooked
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (bovine offal, cooked) is the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for bile-infused hotpot preparations. The rumen-extract broth component is is not separately coded in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Cow Bile Hotpot or its closest equivalent. SNOMED CT does not catalog ethnic specialty hotpot preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Cow Bile Hotpot is approximately 500 g, representing a single-person hotpot portion that includes broth, beef offal pieces, and herbs as served in Guizhou ethnic restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Beef variety meats and by-products, cooked, simmered — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct nutritional entry for Cow Bile Hotpot (牛瘪火锅). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mixed cooked beef offal (tripe, liver, intestines) simmered in broth, weighted to reflect the typical composition of this dish. Sodium is elevated to reflect broth-based preparation with seasoning. The rumen-extract (bile) liquid contributes minimal macronutrients but adds bitter compounds and micronutrients not individually quantified in USDA data. Input AI-generated values (protein 12.5 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g, energy 95 kcal per 100 g) are broadly consistent with USDA cooked beef offal profiles and are retained with medium confidence. Saturated fat estimated from USDA beef offal fatty acid profiles.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. 2008; analogous estimate for high-protein, low-carbohydrate offal broth
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cow Bile Hotpot. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat broth and offal dishes documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Carbohydrate content is 1.2 g per 100 g; glycemic load per 500 g serving = (15 × 6.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.9 GL units. The bitter bile compounds and wild herbs may further moderate postprandial glucose response, consistent with published observations on bitter plant extracts, but this effect is not quantified in the GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 320 mg per 100 g; a 500 g serving delivers approximately 1,600 mg sodium, representing 70% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-salt broth preparation, as the cumulative sodium from the seasoned offal broth can substantially contribute to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) and estimated low GI of 15, combined with high protein (12.5 g per 100 g) that supports satiety and attenuates postprandial glucose excursion.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cow Bile Hotpot is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the low glycemic load (approximately 0.9 per 500 g serving) poses minimal risk of postprandial hyperglycaemia, though sodium intake from the broth should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Offal components (liver, tripe, intestines) are high in dietary cholesterol and purines; sodium content is elevated from broth seasoning, which may adversely affect blood pressure and lipid profiles in susceptible individuals.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish occasionally and in small portions (200 g or less), prioritising lean offal cuts and requesting low-sodium broth, while monitoring overall dietary cholesterol and saturated fat intake.

gout.key_factor: Beef offal (liver, kidney, intestines) is among the highest dietary sources of purines, which are metabolised to uric acid and can precipitate acute gout attacks.
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout or hyperuricaemia should avoid or strictly limit consumption of Cow Bile Hotpot due to the very high purine load from multiple offal components.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cow Bile Hotpot (牛瘪火锅, Niúbiě huǒguō), also known as 'Bie Hotpot' or 'Nvx Biel' in the Miao language, is an ancient and culturally significant specialty of the Miao and Dong ethnic minority groups of Guizhou Province in Southwest China. The defining ingredient is 'Bie' — a liquid extracted by squeezing and filtering the semi-digested grass content from a cow's rumen (first stomach). This greenish-brown liquid is combined with beef offal (tripe, intestines, liver), ginger, garlic, chili, and a blend of wild mountain herbs and spices, then simmered together as a hotpot broth. The resulting dish has a distinctive, powerful bitter taste with a lingering sweet aftertaste, a flavour profile unlike any other Chinese regional cuisine. Nutritionally, Cow Bile Hotpot is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish: approximately 12.5 g protein, 4.5 g fat, and only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, yielding approximately 95 kcal per 100 g. It is rich in B vitamins (particularly B12 at 2.8 mcg, riboflavin at 0.25 mg, and niacin at 4.5 mg), vitamin A (120 mcg RAE), iron (2.5 mg), zinc (1.8 mg), and selenium (12.5 mcg) — all consistent with an offal-dominant dish. Sodium is elevated at 320 mg per 100 g due to broth seasoning. The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), making it suitable for blood sugar management. In Guizhou culture, this dish is considered a high-honour food served to esteemed guests and is traditionally valued for its supposed medicinal properties, including aiding digestion and 'clearing heat' (a concept in Traditional Chinese Medicine referring to detoxification and anti-inflammatory effects). It is typically served at lunch or dinner as a main dish. Clinically, the high purine content from offal is a concern for individuals with gout, and the sodium load warrants caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Cow Bile Hotpot (牛瘪火锅). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for mixed cooked beef offal simmered in broth. The input AI-generated values are broadly consistent with USDA offal profiles and are retained with adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework applied to high-protein, low-carbohydrate offal broth. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No approved source contains a direct entry for Cow Bile Hotpot. All per_100g values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked beef offal profiles. The input AI-generated values (protein 12.5 g, fat 4.5 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g, energy 95 kcal) are consistent with USDA offal data and are retained. Saturated fat (1.6 g) is estimated from USDA beef offal fatty acid profiles. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific bile-infused hotpot preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2024. 3. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat broth and offal dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for this dish. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = 0.9 (very low). 4. ONTOLOGY: No exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Cow Bile Hotpot; approximate matches to beef offal food product (FoodOn FOODON_03315595) and bovine offal cooked (FoodEx2 A00GJ) are used with gap notes. No SNOMED CT code identified. 5. PORTIONS: The input contained two identical duplicate portion entries (both '1 serving (500g)', 500 g); only one is used. 6. HALAL STATUS: Cow bile and rumen extract may not meet halal certification requirements; is_halal is set to false pending formal certification. 7. FOOD GROUP: Reclassified from 'Main dishes' to 'Meat and alternatives' to align with RESULT template food group taxonomy. 8. ALIASES CONSISTENCY: All strings in aliases_by_language are confirmed present verbatim in the flat aliases array.
